data.title: Durable Goods Orders Down 9.3% In June, Less Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

New orders for manufactured durable goods fell 9.3% to $311.85 in June, the largest monthly decline since 2020. The latest reading was just above the projected 10.4% monthly decline. Compared to a year ago, new orders are up 10.9%.

New orders for manufactured durable goods in June, down two of the last three months, decreased $32.1 billion or 9.3 percent to $311.8 billion, the U.S. Census Bureau announced today. This followed a 16.5 percent May increase. Excluding transportation, new orders increased 0.2 percent. Excluding defense, new orders decreased 9.4 percent. Transportation equipment, also down two of the last three months, drove the decrease, $32.6 billion or 22.4 percent to $113.0 billion. (Download full PDF)

Durable Goods

Durable goods refers to tangible products that can be stored or inventoried and that have an average life of at least three years. Durable goods are typically expensive and therefore tend to be purchased when there is confidence in the economy.

New orders for durable goods are a leading indicator, meaning when purchases increase, it typically hints at an improvement to the economy. On the flip side, when the new orders trend down, it is indicating a lack of confidence in the economy.

Economists frequently study this indicator, excluding transportation or defense or both. Just how big are these two subcomponents? Here is a stacked area chart to illustrate the relative sizes over time based on the nominal data. We've also included a dotted line to show the relative size of the core capex subset, which we'll illustrate in more detail below.

The next chart shows the year-over-year percent change in durable goods. We've highlighted the value at recession starts and the latest value for this metric.

If we exclude transportation, "core" durable goods were up 0.2%, higher than the expected 0.1% increase. Core durable goods are up 2.2% from one year ago. The next chart shows the year-over-year percent change in core durable goods.

The next chart shows the growth in core durable goods (purple line) overlaid on the headline number (green dots) since the turn of the century. This overlay helps us see the substantial volatility of the transportation component. Since 2000, durable goods have grown 54.9%, while core durable goods have grown 42.4%.

Durable Goods - Core Capex

Core capital goods new orders are manufacturers' new orders for non-defense capital goods excluding aircraft and is an important gauge of business spending, often referred to as "core capex."

Core capex is company spending that is used to acquire, upgrade, and maintain physical assets such as property, plants, buildings, technology, and equipment. This month, core capex was down 0.7% month-over-month (versus +0.2% forecast) and up 2.8% year-over-year.

The next two charts take a step back in the durable goods process to illustrate core capex. Here is the year-over-year core capex.

The next chart is an overlay of true core capital goods (excluding defense) on the larger series showing the percent change of the two since the turn of the century. Since 2000, durable goods have grown 54.9%, while true core capex has grown 32.3%.

The "Real" Durable Goods Data

In theory, the durable goods orders series should be one of the more important indicators of the economy's health. However, its volatility and susceptibility to major revisions suggest caution in taking the data for any particular month too seriously. Additionally, this series is not adjusted for either population growth or inflation. Let's review the durable goods data with those two adjustments.

In the charts below, the lighter line shows the goods orders divided by the Census Bureau's monthly population data, giving us durable goods orders per capita.

The darker line goes a step further and adjusts for inflation based on the PPI for all commodities, chained in today's dollar value. This gives us the "real" durable goods orders per capita and thus a more accurate historical context in which to evaluate the conventional reports on the nominal monthly data.

We've included a callout in the upper right corner to document the decline of the latest month from the all-time peak for the series.

The next chart is similar to the first one except that it excludes the volatile transportation component; the series is usually referred to as "core" durable goods.

Finally, let's take a big step back in the sales chain and look at the popular series often referred to as "core capex" - nondefense capital goods new orders excluding aircraft (capital goods are durable goods used in the production of goods or services), shown here on a per-capita basis, nominal and real.

The Long-Term Trend in Durable Goods

As these charts illustrate, when we study durable goods orders in the larger context of population growth and also adjust for inflation, the data becomes a coincident macro indicator of a major shift in demand within the U.S. economy.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: MiMedx: Reimbursement Hurdles Mask A Compelling Valuation Opportunity (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
MiMedx Stock Revisited: Transition Continues, But Value Emerges

MiMedx’s stock (NASDAQ:MDXG) has underperformed the broader markets, with returns of -7% versus 50.6% for the S&P 500 since my last evaluation in October 2023. Back then, the company, with an assortment of surgical and wound products, was growing sales 20%+ year-over-year. However, I rated MDXG a Hold and advocated for a “wait-and-see” approach given that the company was mid-transition, with plans to shut down its Regenerative unit.

The paragraphs that follow revisit MiMedx in light of recent developments.

Slowing Growth Amid Reimbursement Delays

Let’s first take a look at MiMedx’s most recent earnings report. The company reported its first-quarter 2025 earnings in late April. Net sales of $88 million represented 4% year-over-year growth. Adjusted EBITDA was $17 million. MiMedx highlighted growth in its Surgical segments (“double-digit growth during the quarter”). On the other hand, it also acknowledged the “further delays” of Local Coverage Determinations, or LCDs. In other words, MiMedx is facing challenges getting reimbursed by Medicare for its skin substitute products (more on this below).

Stable Margins Underpin Improved Profitability

Taking a step back (it’s been almost two years since my last look at MDXG), the company’s net sales have risen from $268 million in 2022 to $321 million in 2023 and $349 million in 2024, representing a compound annual growth rate of ~14%. Meanwhile, profitability has strengthened. The company benefits from consistently high gross margins (~83%), and revenue is outpacing OpEx (e.g., SG&A), implying improved operating leverage.

Strong Clinical Evidence, but Reimbursement Risks Persist

Looking at the bigger picture, MiMedx competes in a competitive and complicated wound market. Its advanced biological grafts do not enjoy broad adoption due to a variety of factors like cost, lack of robust data, and patient/provider preferences. Meanwhile, the FDA has tightened enforcement on human cell and tissue products, prompting MiMedx to pursue formal clinical trials for its products. Due to concerns of overutilization, high costs, and inconsistent evidence of efficacy for many of these products, the Medicare Administrative Contractors required evidence of clinical effectiveness for reimbursement, which Mimedx had been amassing, helping them secure coverage across MAC regions. Recently, the CMS proposed a new payment approach for skin substitutes to “establish 2026 payment rates based on clinical value and relative cost.” The delay of LCDs until January 1, 2026, intertwines with the proposed payment change. Both developments represent CMS's coordinated effort to curtail spending and ensure that only evidence-based skin substitute products are covered.

With a relative abundance of “Level 1 clinical evidence” (randomized controlled trials in this case), MiMedx appears favorably positioned in the placental allograft product market, which is expected to reach $2.4 billion by 2033 (7.3% CAGR). There are also some notable tailwinds in this market. For starters, there is a gradual shift to use these products earlier in treatment protocols as evidence mounts for their effectiveness. Moreover, an aging population results in a higher volume of wounds. According to Markets and Markets, Mimedx held a 33% market share in 2022. Its next closest competitor is Organogenesis (ORGO), which offers wound healing products like PuraPly AM (an antimicrobial skin substitute) and NuShield (placental membrane graft). Outside of specialized firms like MiMedx, large medtech companies like Smith+Nephew, Integra LifeSciences, and Stryker all compete in the wound care market.

Discounted Cash Flow Reveals Undervalued Scenario

I did a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis on MDXG.

As for inputs, $347.9 million in Year 1 Revenue represents the consensus analyst estimate for 2025. 10% revenue growth is a blend of historical data and future expectations. MiMedx’s guidance calls for “annual net sales growth in the low double-digits as a percentage” in the long run. In the trailing twelve months, MiMedx’s free cash flow % (CFO - CapEx / Revenue) was roughly 18. Beta depends on the timeline. Yahoo’s 5Y Monthly beta for the stock is 1.76. Other sources are lower. I felt that 1.5 was the right blend. I also prefer to go with conservative values. In any event, the Monte Carlo simulation will account for some variation in key inputs. Everything else was pretty standard.

Based on my Base case, MDXG is undervalued by 17.43%.

Next, my Sensitivity Analysis reveals what MDXG appears priced for based on its valuation.

Assuming FCF margins of 18%, MDXG appears priced for 6.7% revenue growth on average. This isn’t an unrealistic estimate.

My Monte Carlo Simulation runs 10,000 iterations of my DCF model, so it adjusts for some variability to key inputs like revenue growth, beta, and FCF margin.

So, while MDXG appears undervalued on a cash flow basis based on my inputs, it is not undervalued to a great extent (the current price falls within the 5th and 95th percentiles). So, MDXG could feasibly trade down to $6.39 or up to $10.21 without becoming unusually misvalued (assuming no major changes to its prospects).

Finally, I conducted a Scenario Comparison, which can clue us in on asymmetric opportunity.

The Bear case calls for only 5% revenue growth, so “business as usual,” same reimbursement challenges, minimal market growth, and challenged profitability.

So, from a modeling standpoint, there is a lot of like on MDXG. Critically, it is supported by historical performance and regulatory and market tailwinds.

High-Risk, High-Reward Opportunity Warrants "Buy" Upgrade

To wrap up, while MiMedx’s revenue growth has decelerated, growth in its surgical products and recent regulatory developments impacting its skin products bode well for the future. The company has been consistently profitable in the past couple of years, alleviating concerns of shareholder dilution and permitting the pursuit of growth opportunities, be it in other markets or via M&A. Critically, MiMedx’s evidence-backed portfolio provides a moat should others become more interested in advanced wound care products following more favorable reimbursement. Finally, as implied by my DCF model, MDXG is attractively valued and could offer asymmetric upside should reimbursement tailwinds blow. Given its progress despite reimbursement hurdles, MDXG merits a rating upgrade to Buy. However, make no mistake about it, this is a high-risk/high-reward stock, most appropriate for a barbell portfolio.

Some investment risks include fierce competition, ongoing reimbursement issues, dependence on core products like EpiFix and EpiCord, IP, and legal issues (e.g., the 2019 SEC charges, allegations of questionable sales practices). These risks are probably causing the market to "discount" its stock and could persist.

data.title: Alphabet Is Shifting From An Alpha Bet To Tech's Best Stalwart
data.description:
Since my last Alphabet Inc., aka Google (NASDAQ:GOOG, NASDAQ:GOOGL) analysis, the stock has gained 9.9% in price.

Google posted very strong Q2 2025 results, which is no surprise. Revenue grew by 14% year-over-year, and earnings per share were up 22%. AI Overviews have now substantially transformed the Google Search user experience, with over two billion monthly users globally. And the new "AI Mode" has 100 million monthly active users across the U.S. and India. I think we have a lot more to expect from Google related to AI and Search, and management is slowly—meticulously—laying the groundwork for the internet to continue to be founded on Google Search in the AI age.

One of the core pieces of headline news related to Q2 results is that the company's aggressive capex strategy has expanded. With the new Fiscal 2025 full-year capex target raised to $85 billion (up from $75 billion originally and a significant year-over-year increase from the $52.5 billion in 2024), we can see that management has decided firmly: AI is the race worth winning.

Due to these capital expenditures, operating margins have little room to expand in the very near term, given a 35% year-over-year increase in depreciation, significantly impacted by recent AI infrastructure. But over time, cloud margin expansion toward software-like margins of 30%+ is tangible and clearly part of management's AI strategy.

The capex is also well supported by the heavy operating cash flow of $134 billion as of the last trailing 12 months. Also, the medium-term and long-term return on investment of this spending will likely start to become apparent in 2027/2028 and certainly dominate by 2030, as cloud and AI monetization accelerate faster than depreciation.

The increased capex plan is not just speculative moat building; Alphabet has a record cloud backlog of approximately $106 billion in Q2 2025, representing around 38% year-over-year growth. These contractually obligated future revenues are primarily for large-scale AI and cloud computing workloads, so Alphabet's increase in capex is intended to directly support this (hence the logical and tangible medium-term return on investment). Alphabet Cloud's current annual run rate is about $50 billion as of Q2, so incremental revenue recognition from the $106 billion backlog can realistically push annual cloud revenue toward $70-80 billion by 2027.

Alphabet currently holds about 12% of the global cloud market, so I think with its current backlog and capex commitment it can realistically raise its market share to 15%+ in the medium term, competing more directly with Amazon's AWS (AMZN) and Microsoft Azure (MSFT), which have about a 30% and 22% market share, respectively. That said, it's not like Amazon and Microsoft aren't competing aggressively, creating competitive challenges for Google. AWS has committed to $100 billion of capex in fiscal 2025, and Azure $80 billion.

That said, the near-term drawback of this heavy capex to support AI demand is earnings volatility, even though operating cash flow will continue to look more robust as it is unaffected by depreciation charges. The market is generally intelligent enough to recognize the long-term structural advantages of Google spending heavily on AI now for a lasting (and tangibly cash-flow-evidenced) moat in cloud service demand later.

Valuation Is A Little High, But Still Primed To Buy

When looking at the technical charts, I can see that Google is a little expensive, but it's certainly still investable. The one-week-period price chart shows a 14-week RSI at 62.5, with the price quite substantially higher than the 50-week moving average. From the outset, this means that the stock is being commanded by the bulls and that if you buy Google stock now, it's not as a value investment. Instead, we currently have a growth-at-a-reasonable-price (GARP) investment on our hands with Google stock at this time. The daily chart shows the 14-day RSI at 75 (overbought; expect a pullback soon), and the monthly chart shows a 14-month RSI at 63 (still a little expensive, but not unreasonable).

For long-term investors, don't overthink it. If you want stable, good, durable growth and moderate alpha with reliable returns, then Google is probably just right for you. If you want aggressive alpha and are happy to embrace extreme volatility or have the expertise to trade around such volatility, look past Google; it's not an elite stock pick right now. It's safe—and that's enough for most investors who don't invest professionally full-time.

I conservatively estimate for Fiscal 2027 normalized earnings per share of $12.35 (which is still bullish and represents margin expansion as AI investments begin to pay off). The forward P/E non-GAAP ratio is currently 19.5, but given the likelihood of moderating growth in the medium term due to market saturation and competitive constraints, I expect the multiple to contract somewhat by December 2026 (the charts also indicate this is fair).

At a forward P/E non-GAAP ratio of 18.5 in December 2026 and forward normalized earnings per share of $12.35, I forecast a stock price of $230 in 18 months. The current stock price is $195; the implied upside over 18 months is 18%. Dividends are negligible, and the 12-month return from now will likely be about 10%.

Metric

Value

Normalized EPS (FY 2027)

$12.35

Forward P/E (current, non-GAAP)

19.5

Expected P/E (Dec 2026)

18.5

Forecast Stock Price (Dec 2026)

$230

Current Stock Price

$195

Implied 18-Month Price Return

+18%

Estimated 12-Month Return

~10%

Dividend Yield

Negligible

Sentiment Slowdown Risks From Stalwart-Style Growth

Alphabet's valuation multiples have already shown signs of contraction. For example, the company's five-year average forward P/E non-GAAP ratio of 25 has fallen to just 19.5 today. With normalized earnings growth going to moderate even further over the next three to five years, why would the valuation multiple not contract further? Management likely needs to look at upping the dividend at this juncture; otherwise, sentiment for this stock could wane. The company has certainly increased its share buybacks recently (nearly $60 billion bought back over the last trailing 12 months, compared to $30 billion in 2020), but management could probably do even more on this front moving forward once free cash flow stabilizes from the AI moat.

In other words, Alphabet's investment case is going to change moving forward—but it can still be great with the appropriate stewardship. Thankfully, I have a lot of belief in Pichai, the CEO, and Page and Brin, the founders.

So, with a higher dividend and increased buybacks, Alphabet stock could stay in the 15-20x non-GAAP P/E ratio range long term. But it's still vulnerable and potentially falling into the same category as Apple (AAPL) right now, which is known as a low-growth investment in a strong brand with durable free cash flow. There's nothing wrong with a strong operating company becoming a low-alpha stalwart, but it's just not going to have a place in elite growth portfolios. It definitely doesn't have a place in mine, even though the operations are incredibly admirable. Google is perfect for retirement portfolios, family offices, and other risk-averse funds that seek lower volatility and still want exceptionally reliable capital preservation. The risk here is not in the reliability of total returns; the risk is in the magnitude of such returns.

Conclusion: Buy

You can't bet against Google. Why would you? The company has transformed from the epitome of alpha-seeking into tech's most loved stalwart. The name, Alphabet, is a clever play on "alpha bet", "betting for alpha". I reckon some of my readers would not have known that; it took me some years and market acquaintance to recognize the ingeniousness of the name. Nevertheless, Alphabet is no longer a reliable alpha bet; it's morphing into a stalwart that protects investors from downside and still offers moderate alpha for now.

The big counter to the present low-alpha thesis is that management could engineer much stronger efficiency gains from AI, which may not be fully integrated into the current consensus and is difficult to accurately quantify independently. It is also speculative to assume heavy margin expansion and normalized earnings growth rate expansion based on AI alone in the next three to five years. However, it is possible (and highly probable to occur moderately). Inklings of heavier margin expansion coming to the surface soon, following the heavy capex phase, could certainly lead to Alphabet stock being a reliable Strong Buy again regularly in my book.

data.title: Diversified Healthcare Trust: A REIT In Transition With Hidden Optionality
data.description:
Thesis Summary

DHC is considered a conventional healthcare REIT in the stock market, with a high exposure to volatility in senior housing. This assumption needs some nuance, as DHC is currently in the process of making significant changes financially and structurally to become a platform for value recovery and asset management.

It is important to note that the financial figures of DHC continue to be under pressure; this is mainly because of the large part of non-current debt (page 104). However, as mentioned before, DHC is trying to reduce its debt. Furthermore, the most recent quarterly report shows that there is stabilization in the SHOP business, indicating a new way of working for DHC. These things indicate that there is an opportunity for DHC in areas where there is much land constrained. Opportunities arise if DHC is able to monetize non-core real estate. If so, this could potentially result in enabling new value compared to the traditional business models.

The thesis for this article is therefore based on the assumption that DHC is not only financially (earnings) driven but rather architectural and strategic. Meaning that yesterday’s single CGU would change in tomorrow’s multi-CGU opportunity.

Business Analysis

As described, DHC is a REIT within the healthcare branch. DHC has a real estate portfolio that is diversified over various countries and consists of different types of properties. To put it more concretely, the company had 343 properties in 34 states as of March 31, 2025.

DHC’s main business can be split into two components:

SHOP (Senior Housing Operating Portfolio)

The most revenue of DHC is generated by the SHOP. It is also very volatile, as there is a substantial upside when the occupancy is good. However, it is also extremely sensitive to increases in labor costs and changes in tenants. On a positive note, it should be said that recent operating efficiencies resulted in an increase of 49% (page 3 of the previously linked quarterly report) compared to last year in the SHOP net operating income (NOI). Even more positive is that this increase is expected to remain as the occupancy keeps increasing. The margins, however, are still behind competitors. This is because of the high fixed costs inherent to the real estate sector.

Medical Office & Life Sciences

The second component has a more defensive business model because of the high occupancy rate of 90.1% (page 5 of the previously mentioned report) and long-term leases. The assets are also located near hospitals and academic medical institutes, ensuring stable cashflow for the long term.

Do note that there is an insignificant third business line for DHC, which relates to wellness centers and other healthcare-related buildings that could potentially be sold.

Strengths and Weaknesses

DHC has a geographically diversified portfolio with different classes of properties. This protects the company from regional risks that arise. Through its exposure to life science real estate, medical offices, and senior housing, DHC enjoys a variety of drivers of demand in the aggregate that insulate the company over economic cycles.

Demographics also support DHC's forecast. An aging United States population is fueling long-term demand for senior living and outpatient services—a demographic to which DHC has high exposure. Historical precedent in its Senior Housing Operating Portfolio (SHOP), where NOI rose to 49% YoY, is evidence of early success in tapping the trend.

In addition, most of DHC's portfolio is located in high-value urban and suburban areas where new supply is constrained structurally, giving the company latent optionality with potential redevelopment or sale of assets.

Aside from that, DHC faces some vulnerabilities, though they are manageable. Five Star Senior Living, its largest operator, increases DHC’s operational risk as the company is highly reliant upon it. Its $2.9 billion of debt prevents DHC from making reinvestments and limits its ability to payout dividends. Even after recent improvements, the SHOP segment trails the peer group on size and efficiency. In addition, without a visible external growth pipeline, the company may be halted from sustaining competitive momentum over the next few years.

Strategic Positioning

DHC occupies a niche position in the healthcare REIT segment, created by its operating model, asset platform, and demographic tailwinds. A two-sector core fuels its strategy: senior housing and medical office buildings. In the combination of the two, DHC is able to balance cyclical and non-cyclical cash flows and retain exposure to long-term growth drivers.

DHC's portfolio is geographically diversified but strategically weighted towards high-restraint, supply-constrained suburban and urban markets. Its assets in California, New York, and Massachusetts, while benefiting from the demand density, also benefit from inherent land value significantly in excess of their net book value. This offers strategic optionality to DHC—redevelopment, repositioning, or opportunistic sale possibilities.

Its demographic underpinning of positioning is also robust. According to the U.S. Census Bureau, the 65-and-older population will nearly double in the next three decades. This aging trend is the basis for strong, long-term demand for assisted living and outpatient care, the very industries DHC has focused on exposure. Healthcare delivery, in the meantime, is moving toward decentralized models, driving higher demand for medical office buildings and outpatient facilities in communities.

DHC's historically underperforming SHOP segment at last is starting to show signs of structural reversal. Occupancy and NOI expansion reflect more aggressive positioning relative to peers, particularly with industry supply still constrained by high construction costs and regulatory impediments.

Despite its positioning, DHC will have to continue refining its strategy in order to overcome the disadvantages of scale and cost of capital compared to peers like more sizeable Welltower (WELL) and Ventas (VTR). The extent of value it is able to bring by the selective repositioning of its assets, cautious capital management, and operator diversification will decide the extent to which it is able to translate strategic positioning into shareholder value.

Financials and Balance Sheet

As of Q1 2025, DHC has a total asset value of approximately $4.99 billion against total liabilities valued at $3.05 billion. That gives us a book equity position of $1.95 billion. Healthy top-line numbers, to be sure, but looking closer at the balance sheet reveals areas of concern.

Liquidity is solid, with the company having $306.7 million in cash and equivalents on hand. This buffer is a support to operating flexibility over the short term and a reinvesting or capital-hardening cushion. Nevertheless, the capital structure remains substantially levered: total debt is $2.89 billion with a 6.2-year average life and an average cost of 4.76%. Although the weighted average maturity offers DHC some flexibility, the size of its debt puts constraints on the flexibility of capital, particularly in a rising interest rate scenario.

Operatively, on a cash basis, DHC generated normalized Funds From Operations (FFO) in Q1 2025 of $14.3 million, or $0.06 per share. Tiny as it is, it is welcome news in light of the company's recent history of financial restructuring. But it does not leave much room for error in considering financing capital expenditures or a dividend.

Among the better numbers are SHOP segment results, which generated a NOI of $37 million for the quarter—a 49% increase in relation to last year. That bottom-line momentum will be essential in driving overall FFO and, ultimately, in restoring investor confidence.

Scenario planning suggests that incremental SHOP occupancy and operating margin gains would have a good impact on cash flow, suggesting the importance of investing in that segment.

Valuation

Diversified Healthcare Trust is difficult to estimate given its bifurcated capital structure and asset base. Two different valuation methods will provide complementary insights: FFO multiple benchmarking and the discounted cash flow approach.

The FFO multiple approach values the use of sector competitors. Similar exposure, Healthcare REITs like Sabra Health Care REIT (SBRA) and National Health Investors (NHI) have traded at 11–13x forward FFO multiples in the past. Applying that to DHC's normalized annualized FFO run-rate of around $52–53 million approximates an equity value of $572 million to $686 million, or $2.37 to $2.85 per share.

Whereas FFO identifies relative market attitude, a DCF model determines long-term intrinsic value. Based on a normalized yearly FFO of $60 million with a conservative 2.5% compound annual growth and 9% discount rate, I estimated a terminal value of around $945 million. Discounted for five years, such a terminal value is equal to around $614 million in present value. And, adding the value of the present FFO produced over the five-year projection time horizon (~$225 million), the enterprise value stands at approximately $839 million.

Net debt is, though, $2.58 billion. Subtracting this from enterprise value, the implied equity value based on conservative assumptions turns out to be negative. This difference implies that while DHC produces recurring cash flow, its capital structure reduces intrinsic equity value significantly.

A scenario-based valuation explains this difference as well:

Base Case

FFO flat at ~$60M annually, in line with Q1 2025's run rate; assuming consistent occupancy and no margin compression: ~$2.60/share (mid of peer multiple range)

Upside Case

Assumes 25% incremental growth in SHOP NOI from incremental increase in occupancy and modest rate growth to drive normalized FFO to ~$75M: ~$3.15/share

Downside Case

Assumes flattening, or modest decline in SHOP and MOB performance to FFO of ~$50M annually: ~$2.00/share

The DCF methodology makes heavy use of the sensitivity of equity value and capital efficiency. The FFO basis has much more positive peer-relative positioning. Together, they suggest that DHC's equity is close to fair market value, but with huge underlying optionality related to deleveraging and operating improvement.

Risks

Even with early signs of improved operations, DHC possesses certain crucial risks that can disrupt its ability to generate long-term shareholder returns.

First among them is that it has high leverage. With nearly $2.9 billion in debt outstanding, DHC's financial flexibility is limited. That high debt level is beyond its capacity to reinvest in the portfolio, raise distributions, or take advantage of strategic growth prospects. Refinancing at higher rates costs more, and this can reduce margins and shorten refinancing horizons.

Another key risk is the company's concentration in Five Star Senior Living, as it represents 118 of the company's communities. Any underperformance of Five Star on either the operating or financial side would disproportionately impact DHC's performance. Such reliance curtails operating flexibility as well as introduces more volatility.

As far as short-term liquidity is concerned, although DHC has refinanced part of its 2025 maturities for $380 million, it still has to finance obligations in the form of secured debt due in 2026. Depending on market conditions and credit spreads, this can result in either rising borrowing costs or asset sales to raise capital.

In addition, the healthcare real estate sector is sensitive to regulatory changes by nature. If Medicare or Medicaid reimbursement rates change, or there are changes in labor law or compliance practices—particularly with staffing requirements in elder living—this can have a devastating impact on operator performance and operating margins. Long-term care regulatory trends are dynamic, much more so than the flexibility of this REIT to shift its strategy or holdings.

Finally, DHC's deleveraging plan also partially relies on timely asset sales. Nevertheless, in adverse market conditions, there are possibilities of selling assets below NAV or delaying sales, thus delaying its balance sheet normalization.

Conclusion

DHC shows improvement in its operating metrics, and near-term refinancing risk appears alleviated. However, the REIT remains in transition with greater leverage, a structurally distressed operating partner, and limited external growth drivers.

I feel that patient investors can monitor for SHOP NOI growth and occupancy rebound, efforts to reposition assets or re-tenanting, and debt reduction and FFO stability at $60M or more.

My target Price Range is $2.50–$3.20 (12-month view)

Recommendation: Hold

data.title: Costco: Reiterating My Short As Immigration Crackdown And Tariffs Bite
data.description:
It’s been a year since I published my original COST short thesis, and if you’ve been following along, you know I argued it was worth about half its market value. Back then, people thought I was crazy for shorting America’s beloved warehouse. COST was the golden child of consumer staples, a company that trades at sky-high multiples despite virtually no reason behind it. But here we are today, and I find myself reiterating my short – now with even more conviction than before.

COST is a phenomenal business from an operational point of view. They have loyal members, a streamlined model, and an incredible negotiating power with suppliers. Charlie Munger called them one of the greatest retail models in existence. But great companies aren’t always great stocks, especially when valuations get completely detached from reality.

When I wrote my short last year, COST was trading at over 50x earnings, a multiple that made little sense for a business with no real margin expansion. My thesis was simple back then: COST is expensive, it doesn’t have a robust online business, and its EBIT margins have hovered at 3-4% forever. Now, however, the thesis has an entirely new leg to stand on—and it’s coming straight from Washington, DC.

Immigration

Last month, President Trump escalated his immigration crackdown. ICE raids targeting day laborer hotspots like Home Depot parking lots have started to make waves and have alerted the Hispanic communities across the country. Deportation fears are skyrocketing, and spending patterns are changing in some cases overnight.

A recent Kantar report found that in-store shopping among Hispanic consumers has fallen over 11% YoY. Supermarkets are seeing the biggest drops.

That matters for Costco. Many of its highest-volume locations are in states with large Hispanic populations—California, Texas, Arizona, and Nevada—and its bulk, in-person retail model depends on frequent store visits. In fact, Hispanic consumers accounted for almost 40% of Costco’s traffic growth in a recent survey by Numerator. That tells you how core this demographic is to Costco’s in-person model, which depends on high-volume, high-frequency visits.

Other retailers are already seeing it: Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), and even beverage companies like Coca-Cola (KO) and Constellation Brands (STZ)—brands include Modelo and Corona—have flagged a Hispanic consumer pullback.

Jim Sabia, president of STZ's beer division, described a noticeable drop in Hispanic foot traffic at California retail locations that were once consistently busy. “There is a fear of the ICE raids,” he said. “There is a fear out there, so these consumers are changing their behavior.”

Costco won’t be the exception here—it just hasn’t shown up in the numbers yet.

Tariffs

If the immigration headwind wasn’t enough, COST itself dropped a quiet bombshell in its Q3 2025 filing. They wrote:

Government actions in various countries relating to tariffs affect the costs of some of our merchandise. The degree of our exposure is dependent on (among other things) the type of goods, rates imposed, and timing of the tariffs. Higher tariffs are more likely to adversely impact rather than improve our results.

In typical COST understated language, that’s a pretty loud alarm bell. Tariffs are going up, and their exposure is broad. Costco isn’t exactly known for nimble pricing adjustments. Their whole business model is built around low margins and high volume. They sell cheap in bulk and make their money off scale. But tariffs directly raise their cost of goods sold. If they pass that onto customers, their legendary low prices take a hit. If they don’t pass it on, their margins, which are already razor thin, would get squeezed even further. A lose-lose situation.

The Same Old Margin Problem

Let’s rewind to what I said a year ago, because it’s still true today. COST’s EBIT margins have been stuck in the 3-4% range for years. Investors keep hoping for an expansion to 5-6%, but it never comes, and I think it'll never come. Why? Because Costco isn’t Apple or Google. They don’t sell software with near-zero marginal cost. They sell gallons of mayonnaise and 60 packs of toilet paper. There’s no margin magic wand here.

Their business is phenomenal at what it does, but unless they raise prices dramatically—and risk customer defections to Walmart, Target, or local grocers—margin expansion is unlikely. The market has priced in perpetual perfection. COST trades at a premium multiple above 40x, at times over 50x, despite being a grocery and consumer staple retailer operating in an industry that typically trades at 12-16x earnings.

Risks to the Short Thesis

COST remains beloved by investors, especially the passive ones. It has a powerful moat, and passive ownership by massive asset managers keeps shares out of trading circulation, which somewhat reduces the likelihood of a down pull.

Also, as mentioned earlier, Trump could back down on tariffs completely or pause them in a pivot, as he has done in the past. That would relieve some cost pressure on COST. Tariffs or no tariffs, Costco still faces an immigration-driven spending slowdown and stagnant EBIT growth.

Another real risk with this short is timing. Even if the thesis is fundamentally right, it could take years for the market to finally price it in. You’re still paying borrower fees and interest on the borrowed shares. Carrying this position isn’t cheap.

Final Thoughts

The picture is quite simple, in my opinion. This is a retailer with stagnant EBIT growth, limited pricing power, and new macro headwinds it cannot control. Yet it trades at a PE multiple higher than any of its direct and indirect competitors.

I said it last year, and I’ll say it again, louder this time: COST is worth about half its current value. And now, with immigration crackdowns and tariffs entering the equation, I believe this short is more justified than ever before.

At the end of the day, great companies can be bad stocks. COST is exactly that.

data.title: Atkore: Stabilization Around The Corner
data.description:
In the fall of last year, I concluded that stabilization seemed to arrive in the case of Atkore Inc. (NYSE:ATKR), as that conclusion has turned out to be a bit premature. Shares plunged at the time, with margins reverting towards more normal levels in a post-pandemic environment.

The margin reversal now seems complete, as investors have hopes that no negative overshoot takes place. If the company can stabilize margins here, and grow from here onwards, current valuations look quite compelling. A flattish performance allows for real valuation multiple inflation over time, as I am happy to hold onto a modest long position.

Protecting & Powering The World

Atkore is a producer and distributor of a range of electrical, safety and infrastructure solutions. Products to think of include PVC products, infrastructure products, cable & cable management trays, electrical conduits, fittings, among others.

The company claims a good exposure to megatrends, which include trends like renewable energy, grid hardening, electrification of everything and increased digital infrastructure.

This in theory is a very sound positioning, as a somewhat specialized building material provider, yet the company enjoyed a huge boom in the post-pandemic environment, with a substantial pullback seen in more recent times.

Just a $1.5 billion business in 2017, the company saw revenues explode to nearly $4 billion in the fiscal year 2022, with sales having corrected towards $3 billion by now. Historical operating margins, which came in at levels around 10%, peaked around 30% during the 2022 boom, but have now normalized again.

Looking at that boom and subsequent fall gives a distorted picture in isolation, as Atkore has been an extremely aggressive buyer of its own shares, with the share count cut nearly in half over the past decade!

Reversion Continues

During the peak year of 2022, the company reported sales of $3.9 billion, with operating profits topping $1.2 billion, which at the time yielded earnings of around $20 per share. It was very clear that this was not sustainable as 2023 sales fell by 10%, as volumes declines were even worse. Operating profits were down 30% to nearly $900 million, still marking very healthy margins.

The company originally guided for flattish sales between $3.50-$3.65 billion in 2024, with shares peaking at a high around $190 early in the year. After a 4% fall in first quarter sales, second quarter sales were down 11% causing a massive correction in the share price. This was not aided by another 10% fall in third quarter sales, and an unexpected CFO resignation over the summer.

While both sales and margins were moving down, earnings were still trending at a run rate of around $13-$15 per share, as net debt of $460 million was manageable given that EBITDA was still reported in excess of $800 million. Nonetheless, continued pressure on the results was seen, while investors were furthermore facing a potential overhang of antitrust lawsuits.

Believing that the business should still post earnings in excess of $5 per share based on a normalized $3 billion revenue base and 10% operating margins, an $85 stock looked attractively priced in September. This also was the case as the company guided for EBITDA at a minimum of $650 million in 2025.

Still Struggling

Since the fall of last year, shares of Atkore have temporarily risen to the $100 mark around the time of the U.S. election, fell to just $50 during the tariff rout in the spring, and have now recovered to $78 per share, down minimally compared to September of last year.

Fiscal 2024 sales ended up down 9% to $3.20 billion with operating profits falling further to $625 million, cut in half from the peak, but still representing margins around 20% of sales! This meant that full year earnings power reported at $12 and change, yet fourth quarter profits were stock around two dollars.

The normalization was set to continue in 2025, with sales seen down to $2.9-$3.2 billion, but moreover EBITDA seen down further to a midpoint of half a billion, which would largely result in earnings between $8 and $9 per share, still above the long-term normal margins estimated.

In February, first quarter sales were reported down 17%, with the midpoint of the guidance cut by another hundred million to $400 million, resulting in earnings somewhere around $6 per share, this being in line with my long term average. Despite the dismal results, including a big impairment charge communicated for its HDPE assets, the company announced a penny increase in the quarterly dividend by the end of April, payable at $0.33 per share.

In May, second quarter sales were reported down 11% as the company maintained the full year guidance. That is very welcomed, with net debt of $436 million now surpassing the estimated EBITDA performance this year, for a leverage ratio just over 1 times.

Continued buybacks make that the share count has come down to 34 million shares, which at $78 grants the business a $2.7 billion equity valuation, and just over $3.1 billion enterprise valuation. This is equal to only 2.5 times the peak operating profits for 2022! With earnings now trending around $6 per share, the normalization in margins seems rather complete, as the company posts earnings around $6 per share based on the current performance, in line with my thoughts last year, as the normalization of margins seems complete.

If earnings no longer come down, the current earnings power yields a mere 13 times earnings multiple, which is very reasonable, but likely comes with skepticism on the earnings power here, and also likely fears that margins normalization might overshoot towards the negative.

What Now?

The reality is that I am growing quite upbeat about Atkore Inc., even as margins have fully normalized here in my view. Stability seems to be within reach, but for that we have to see year-over-year growth again. Tariffs might actually provide a tailwind, as the share of imported PVC has risen in recent years, and stricter regulations might help domestic players like Atkore as foreign peers are slapped with tariffs.

This makes me quite upbeat here, as leverage is manageable as these levels. Frankly, the biggest opportunity is that of stabilization of the business, with shares now trading at a low-teens earnings multiple, as a return to growth might drive a real valuation multiple inflation, certainly given the continued megatrends driving volume growth in the business in the long haul.

In such a case, Atkore Inc. shares might easily recover to triple digits again, with real potential for optimism driven moves having the potential to push up shares to higher levels in between. All this lays the groundwork for much better days to come, so I feel comfortable to hold a position at current levels.

data.title: HCA: This Healthcare Giant Will Not Make Your Portfolio Sick
data.description:
Introduction

It was the night before COVID. Stocks were falling globally as the virus spread. What was an investor to do? Sell and risk losing out in a recovery if the virus were contained? Buy and risk further mark-to-market losses as the death toll rose? In early March 2020, we had the perfect heads I win, tails the virus loses solution: buy HCA Healthcare (HCA), the largest hospital operator in the US. If COVID dissipated, the market and multiples would rise, lifting all boats. If the epidemic became a pandemic, hospitals would be full, and HCA’s revenues would rise.

There turned out to be one minor hitch in our best-laid plans: politicians. Before HCA and other hospitals got an influx of COVID patients, politicians decided that they needed to be prepared. So they decreed an end to all elective procedures to keep capacity free. These are the most profitable part of a hospital’s operations. A quick in and out, and you can charge more than the Four Seasons does for a night. Also, a hospital’s costs tend to be largely fixed. Doctors and nurses still have to be paid even if they are twiddling their thumbs, depreciation is on auto-pilot, and interest on debt keeps accruing. So a ban on elective procedures was devastating to hospital financials, and HCA’s stock dropped further.

Industry Structure

At this point, it is important to understand the structure of the hospital industry in the US. Most hospitals are non-profits, with only 20% being for-profit. As non-profits, they pay out all the revenues they get in a given year, with perhaps a minor profit or loss. In addition, if you’d like a wing named after you, they’ll gladly take your millions. But non-profits have nowhere to turn to when they are in financial distress. No shareholders to tap, no dividend to cut. They could take on debt, but in a distressed situation, this is hard. The only entity they can appeal to is the government. Local hospitals are a powerful lobbying group. Perhaps the Senator from the area had his heart bypass operation recently, or the Cabinet member from the area had her hip replaced. They are also large employers of educated, politically active people. So, when the head of a hospital calls a politician, you can be sure the call is taken.

The COVID giveaways began flowing, and the hospital industry was promised billions for their public service. HCA, with the largest number of beds in the US, received its fair share. At the same time, COVID surged, hospitals filled up, and revenue spiked. Notwithstanding the human toll, it was a good time to be a hospital company. HCA’s stock, which was $127 when we recommended it, had fallen to $80 but quickly recovered and was at $190 a year later. To its credit, HCA returned all the extra government money it got. (The non-profits? They pocketed everything. It was a good time to be a travel nurse!)

We say all this to make one very important point: the majority of US hospitals being non-profits while providing an essential service means that politicians will ensure that the industry will never face continuing financial distress.

HCA Healthcare Financial Overview and Outlook

HCA’s recently reported results were very good. For the quarter ending June 30, 2025, the company reported a 6% increase in revenues and a 12% rise in net income, with some benefit from a lower tax rate. The company shrank its share count by 8% YoY, leading to a 23% increase in EPS.

The company ended the quarter with $0.9 billion of cash and $44.5 billion of debt. While the absolute amount of debt is high, it is manageable. Interest accounts for 20% of operating income, which is largely stable due to the nature of the business.

Free cash flow was in line with net income. The company spent a small amount on acquisitions and returned all its profits to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases.

The company raised its EPS guidance for the year from approximately $25 to $26. We regard this as eminently achievable.

HCA Valuation: Fair Value of $416

To value the stock, we apply a below-market multiple of 16x on 2025 EPS of $26 to arrive at a $416 fair value. A 16x multiple would imply a 6% earnings yield, which we regard as reasonable for a high-quality, stable business, given the current 10-year Treasury yield of 4.4%. Along with the 1% dividend yield, we thus see a potential for a 25% total return over the next year from the current $335 stock price.

The company is slated to earn $28 per share next year. In a bear case, the company’s earnings will come in lower than expected in the $25 range. A depressed 12x multiple would result in a $300 stock price, or 10% downside.

Taking the above two scenarios into consideration, we see a favorable 2.5:1 reward/risk ratio.

The company’s main competitors are non-profits and not publicly traded. The publicly traded competitors are not as well-managed and have higher levels of debt. Tenet Healthcare (THC) trades at 10x EPS and has consistently been at a lower valuation.

We recommend that investors with a diversified portfolio buy HCA shares. The stock’s implied volatility is high, and the return can be supplemented by selling covered calls, perhaps at the $400 strike.

External Ratings

Seeking Alpha’s quant rating system rates the stock a hold, with an overall rating of 3.4 out of 5. It garners a surprising D+ for valuation, seemingly driven by its low dividend yield. Wall Street analysts are not surprisingly more positive, with an average rating of 4.0, equating to a buy. The average target price is $392.

Our Contrarian View

Hospital stocks have recently taken a beating after the passage of the One, Big, Beautiful Bill, which envisages cuts to Medicaid spending. There is concern that this lower spending will result in lower revenues for healthcare providers like hospitals. We believe this concern is overblown. States will fill some of the federal cutbacks. People who lose Medicaid may obtain private insurance through their employer or insurance exchanges. Prices may rise to account for more unreimbursed care. At the end of the day, a non-profit hospital that operates at break-even will remain in that state. For-profit hospitals that generate a margin due to their relative efficiency will continue to do so.

Moderate Risks

The biggest risk here is that the company’s earnings will come in lower than expected due to lower government funding of healthcare that is not offset.

The company depends on its employees doing a competent job and needs to maintain good relations with them with regular pay raises.

Shareholders depend on a company’s management being good stewards of their capital. Although the company has been prudent in its acquisition strategy so far, this is not assured.

Lower immigration and higher deportations could result in a lower pool of available patients in the US.

Healthcare advances, including GLP-1 drugs, could make the population vastly healthier, resulting in lower hospitalizations. Or maybe you believe RFK, Jr. will MAHA!

Sentiment towards the hospital sector could get worse, resulting in depressed valuations.

Conclusion

An investment in HCA offers an opportunity to buy the best managed hospital chain in the US for a reasonable price, with the potential for a 25% return over the next year. The company provides an essential service that is not affected by economic conditions. The company has been an aggressive buyer of its stock, and we expect it to continue to shrink its share count, which has gone down by 25% over the last five years.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Contrarian Idea investment competition, which runs through July 28. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Eastern Bankshares: Growing Through Acquisitions, Attractive Value
data.description:
We continue our critical Q2 earnings season review of regional bank performance today with Eastern Bankshares, Inc. (NASDAQ:EBC). Thus far, regional banks have had better-than-expected earnings. We like examining regional banks to get a better understanding of what is happening with local economies on the ground. On average, loans and deposits are growing, margins have stabilized and are higher than historical averages, the cost of funds is coming down, and we have a more friendly administration for banks here in 2025. Thus far, it has been a great earnings season.

We follow Eastern Bankshares, and this one is a bit unique in that it has grown through mergers and thereby increased its loan and deposit base inorganically. Most recently, it is bringing HarborOne Bancorp (HONE) into the fold.

All things considered, the key metrics here are strong, and we do like the acquisitions being made for the long term, so we think shares are a long-term buy here. Let us discuss the just-reported Q2 earnings.

Eastern Bankshares' Q2 sees strong headline results

The top line was up big from last year, coming as no surprise due to its merger activity that we mentioned above. Here in Q2, revenues were $244.9 million, up 59% from last year. This was, however, a beat on estimates of $3.6 million. Let us dig into the key metrics, which we view as mixed here relative to the sequential Q1. Deposits ticked up 2% here to $21.22 billion, versus $20.80 billion at the end of Q1. Loans were also up 2% sequentially to $18.59 billion versus $18.2 billion in Q1. Deposits grew primarily due to increases in municipal government deposits.

How about margins? For Eastern Bankshares, the bank saw a very strong 21 basis point expansion on net interest margin to 3.59% from 3.38% in the sequential quarter. Further, the margins are up 95 basis points from a year ago. This is very strong. This led to an expansion in net interest income to $202.0 million from $188.9 million. Non-interest income also expanded to $42.9 million. Factoring in operating expenses, we saw a nice increase in net income. Net income was $81.7 million, or $0.41 per share, compared to net income of $67.5 million, or $0.34 per share, in Q1. This was a solid beat of $0.03 versus estimates. How about asset quality?

Eastern Bankshares' asset quality

There was only a minor provision for credit losses. In Q2, Eastern Bankshares had a provision for credit losses of $7.6 million, but this was up from $6.6 million in Q3. Eastern Bank shares also slightly increased the allowance for credit losses to $232.1 million from $224.3 million. That was an increase from 1.25% of total loans to 1.27% of total loans. That said, non-performing loans were just 0.30% of total loans versus 0.51% in Q1. So this is an improvement. Further, net charge-offs were actually a credit this quarter of $0.2 million in recoveries compared to $11.2 million of charge-offs in Q1, or 0.26% of average total loans. That is also positive.

On top of this, the return metrics are all improving from Q1. The adjusted return on average assets was 1.30%, a significant improvement from 1.09% in Q1. Further, the operating return on tangible equity surged to 13.56% from 11.70% in the sequential quarter. This was driven by a considerable improvement in operating efficiency. The operating efficiency ratio improved to 50.8%, a 290 basis point improvement from 53.7% last quarter, and the best this metric has been in many quarters.

Valuation and shareholder-friendly

One reason that we see a soft Buy rating as appropriate is that while there is a lot of uncertainty now with another, and rather larger, merger with HarborOne, the valuation is getting attractive. Without considering that future merger, the stock is trading at a discount to its book value and only a slight premium to its tangible book value. At $15.9 per share, EBC stock is cheap versus the $17.42 book value and attractive relative to the 12.53 tangible book value. Of course, most banks we have covered are seeing significant premiums right now, so we think there is an opportunity here.

Further, the bank pays a dividend of $0.13 per share, or over 3% yield. On top of that, it is buying back shares. Eastern repurchased 183,053 shares in the quarter at an average price of $16.36 for an aggregate purchase price of $3.0 million.

Take-home

There is uncertainty with yet another merger coming, but this inorganic growth has been paying off. Margins are stronger. Earnings power is up. There is a nice dividend. Asset quality is mostly positive. Repurchases always help, and the stock relative to book value is attractive. We think EBC makes for one to consider for a long-term investment.
